deityNote.Kelemvor: Judge of the Dead — reformed the Wall; his church guards it.
deityNote.Jergal: The Scribe of the Dead, who kept the ledgers before Kelemvor.
deityNote.Myrkul: The Lord of Bones, of the Dead Three — feared and reviled.
deityNote.Oghma: Lord of Knowledge — the Binder of What Is Known.
deityNote.None: The Faithless — you serve no god, and the Wall is built for souls like yours.

conversations.title: The Threshold
conversations.blurb: A gatekeeper of Kelemvor's church bars the causeway to the Wall. He reads your blood, your god, your bearing — and decides. Every line below changes with who you are.

returned.text: *(Your dead-touched senses reach the man and find him layered like a grave: the grey-and-bone of his office on the surface, and beneath it, pressed close and cold, the thing he stands guard over. He is not afraid of *you.* He is afraid *for* you — and of the Wall at his back, which has, lately, begun to look at *him.* Six years he has kept this gate. Something on the other side has finally learned his name.)*

variants.text: *(A Justiciar in the grey-and-bone of Kelemvor's church bars the causeway, halberd grounded. His eyes find you and go still in a way you have learned to recognize.)* You came up the causeway with no god's light on you at all. *(softly, not unkindly)* I can always tell. The air doesn't bend around the Faithless the way it bends around the rest — it just... lets you through, like it's already given up. So you know what's waiting at the end of this Wall, then. And you walked toward it anyway. *(He studies you.)* Brave, or stupid, or grieving. Which is it?

*(The Justiciar's hand is on his blade before his mind has finished the thought.)* You wear the Lord of Bones' mark. Openly. *Here.* At the threshold of the god who *unseated* him. *(His knuckles whiten on the haft.)* You have either lost your mind, pilgrim, or you have come a very long way to lose your head.

*(The Justiciar's gaze drops to the sigil you carry, then rises to his own, at his throat — the same balance, the same grey.)* Kelemvor's mark. *(Some of the iron goes out of his shoulders, and something more tired takes its place.)* The Judge sends me a kinsman, on the worst night I have stood this post. Be welcome, then — though I warn you, welcome here is a thin and frightened thing tonight.

*(His eyes track the horns, the tail, the old reflex of a man taught from boyhood what your blood is supposed to mean.)* ...They posted the order to turn away anything with the Hells in its veins. *(He does not raise the halberd. Quite.)* But they post a great many orders up here, and I've stopped trusting half of them. State your business, fiend-blood, and we'll see which teaching holds tonight — theirs, or my own eyes.

*(He takes in the obsidian skin, the white hair, the surface-stranger's squint against even this dim torchlight.)* A child of the Underdark. On the surface. At a *holy* threshold. *(He exhales through his nose.)* You are a very long way from the dark, traveler — and I am a very long way from trusting anything that comes out of it. Convince me.

*(He marks the long-lived stillness in your face, the eyes that have outlasted men like him by lifetimes.)* You've the elder look. The eyes that have watched a Wall like this one rise, and crack, and be mortared up again, across years I'll never see the back of. *(grimly)* So you already know how this ends, don't you. Tell me why you've come anyway.

*(A Justiciar in the grey-and-bone of Kelemvor's church bars the causeway, halberd grounded, his face like a closed and balanced ledger.)* Hold. The Threshold is shut past dusk, by the Doomguide's own order. *(He looks you over, weighing.)* State your name, your business, and your god — in that order. The last one matters most, up here.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He waits, and the wind off the Wall carries a sound up the causeway — voices, perhaps, if voices could be worn that thin, that long.)* Well? The night isn't getting any younger. *(a dry, joyless flicker)* And neither, godly or otherwise, are you.

choices.text: [ELF] "I stood on this causeway when the first stones of your Wall were laid, Justiciar. I have watched what it does to the dead across centuries you will never live to count. Stand aside, and let an elder pass."
[CLERIC] "I tend the dead too. Ground the halberd and speak to me as one grave-keeper to another — we both know it's the only honest way to speak up here."
[ACOLYTE] "I took the same vows you did, in a chapter house not so different from yours. I know the words behind your orders — and I know the doubt behind the words."
[KELEMVOR] "By the Judge we both serve, you know I have a right to walk this causeway. Don't make us both stand here pretending otherwise."

[FAITHLESS] "I serve no god, Justiciar. Which makes me the one soul on this causeway who knows, personally, exactly what your Wall is for. Look at me — and tell me I haven't earned the right to look at it."
[MYRKUL] *(let the cold of the Bone Lord answer for you, and say nothing at all)*
[INTELLIGENCE] "The Doomguide's dusk-order rests on the Third Codicil — which expressly exempts pilgrims of grief. I am one. Consult your own canon before you bar me on a misreading of it."
[STRENGTH] *(set a hand to the haft of his halberd, unhurried, and let him feel that you could simply take it from him)* "I would rather not. But this Threshold opens tonight, one way or another."
[PERSUASION] "You do not want to be the man who turned away the one soul who could have helped, on the night it mattered. I can see that fear on you already. So let me by, and be free of it."
[INSIGHT] "You keep glancing back over your shoulder, at the Wall. You're not guarding it from me, Justiciar. You're guarding *me* from *it.* What is happening up there tonight?"
[INTIMIDATION] "Look at me, and then look at how alone you are out here. The next thing across this causeway will be far less polite than I am being. Stand. Aside."
[LAWFUL] "There is a proper form for a pilgrim's petition at a closed threshold, and you and I both know it. Let us observe it, by the book, and waste no more of the night."
"Just let me through. Please. I haven't the strength left tonight for a riddle at a gate."

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He is quiet a moment, and when he answers, the bluster has gone out of him — replaced by the particular humility of a short-lived man before a long-lived one.)* ...Three ages. *(He looks at the Wall as though seeing it through your eyes, and not liking the view.)* I've stood this post six years and called myself its keeper. You watched it *born.* *(He grounds the halberd, deliberately, point to stone.)* Then you'll know I'm not the one keeping you out — the *order* is. But an elder who's seen it rise and fall has standing I can't pretend away. *(quietly)* Tell me why you've come, and I'll find a way to let the canon agree with me.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(Something in his shoulders eases — the relief of a lonely man finding one of his own kind on a bad night.)* One grave-keeper to another, then. *(He lowers the halberd.)* You know the part they never write in the recruiting liturgies — that tending the dead is mostly tending the *living's* terror of them. *(He glances back at the Wall.)* And tonight the dead are not being quiet, friend, and I have no liturgy for that. *(He meets your eyes.)* If you truly keep graves, maybe you've a word I don't. Come — but come knowing what you walk toward.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He searches your face, and finds the thing only the initiated carry.)* ...The vows. *(He almost smiles, and it almost reaches his eyes.)* Then you know the second half of every one of them is a doubt the chapter house never let you say aloud. *(He exhales.)* I have been saying mine aloud, lately. Out here. To the wind. *(He steps half-aside.)* A brother in the doubt is worth more to me tonight than a brother in the certainty ever was. Tell me your business plainly, and I'll trust the telling.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He touches his sigil, then yours, the old recognition between servants of the same hard master.)* A right. *(He breathes out.)* Yes. You have one, and I'd be a poor servant to make you fight me for it. *(He lowers the halberd entirely.)* The truth is I'm glad of you. The Judge's work tonight is past one man, and the church sent me *one man.* *(He looks back at the Wall, and his voice drops.)* Something is wrong with it, kinsman. Wrong in a way the canon doesn't have a page for. Walk up with me, and tell me if your eyes read it the way mine do.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(For a long moment he says nothing. Then, slowly, he does the one thing his order forbids at this post — he really *looks* at you, not as a category, but as a person.)* ...Earned the right. *(quietly)* No one's ever put it to me like that. They come up here godless and *terrified*, begging or lying about a faith they don't hold. You came up godless and *angry*, and told me the truth. *(He wrestles with it visibly.)* My order says you're already condemned. My eyes say you're the most honest soul I've met on this causeway in six years. *(He grips the halberd, undecided.)* So tell me, Faithless — and tell me true, because you've spent your one coin of honesty already — *why* have you come to the very Wall that's waiting to take you?

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "Because you're afraid, and a frightened gatekeeper means the thing he's guarding has changed. I came to see what the Wall is becoming — before it becomes it to me."
"Because someone I loved is *in* it. Unworshipping, unworthy, unmourned by anyone with the right to mourn — so I came to do it without the right. Bar me for that, if your god lets you."
[DECEPTION] "To pray, Justiciar. To find a god, here at the end, before the Wall finds me first. Isn't that what you'd want to hear?"

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(The anger he was braced for never comes. Instead he looks, briefly, like a man remembering his own dead.)* ...To mourn without the right. *(He is silent.)* My order would call that the first sin and the last one. *(He looks at the Wall, then back at you, and makes a decision that costs him.)* My order can write me up for it in the morning. *(He stands aside.)* Go and mourn your nameless one, Faithless. Someone should. The canon never could explain to me why grief should need a license — and I have stopped pretending it can.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He wants to believe it so badly that he does.)* To find a god at the end. *(He nods, slowly, the lie warming a cold place in him.)* That's the only ending this Wall ever made me hope for. *(He stands aside.)* Then go and look for one, and may you find it before the dusk-order's reasons catch up to either of us. *(He does not see the corner of the truth you kept folded in your hand. Few do.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(His face closes like a ledger snapping shut.)* No. *(flatly)* You spent your honesty, Faithless, and then you tried to pass me a counterfeit coin on the change. *(He levels the halberd.)* A godless soul I might have pitied. A godless *liar* at Kelemvor's own threshold, I will not pass. Turn around. Walk back down the causeway. And be grateful the only thing I'm taking from you tonight is the road.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He blinks. Then, despite himself, the ghost of respect crosses his face — a scholar's salute to a sharper scholar.)* ...The Third Codicil. *(He actually pauses to check it against his memory, and finds you right.)* Exemption for pilgrims of grief. *(He huffs out something that isn't quite a laugh.)* Six years I've stood this gate and never once had a petitioner out-canon me on it. *(He grounds the halberd.)* You've read deeper than the men who gave me the order. That's either very reassuring or very alarming, and tonight I'll take reassuring. Tell me your business — properly, since you clearly know the proper forms.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He does not flinch — but he does not pretend, either. He feels the truth in your grip and weighs it like a man who has lost fights before and learned from each.)* ...You could. *(evenly)* I'll grant you that, and I'll not insult us both by acting otherwise. *(His jaw tightens.)* But understand what it'll mean. You take this gate by force, you don't pass as a pilgrim — you pass as the first violence the Wall's seen tonight, and the Wall is *hungry* for that tonight in a way I can't explain to you. *(He meets your eyes.)* So choose. Take it, and we both find out what you've fed. Or take your hand off the haft, and let me find you a reason the canon can live with.

choices.text: *(Tighten your grip. Take the gate.)* "Then let's both find out."
*(Take your hand off the halberd, slowly.)* "...Find me your reason, then. I'm listening."

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(The fear you named lands true, and you watch him decide he would rather carry a reprimand than that particular regret.)* ...The man who turned you away. *(He grimaces.)* Gods help me, you're right — that's the exact ghost I've been arguing with all night. *(He stands aside.)* I'd sooner answer to the Doomguide for opening the gate than to myself for shutting it on the one who'd have mattered. Go up. *(lower)* And if you *are* that one — make me right.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(Your approach slides off him like rain off oiled leather. He's heard this shape of words before, and his face doesn't move.)* No. *(not unkind, but immovable)* That was a *practiced* thing you just did — the lean, the eyes, the little hook at the end. I've had it tried on me by better, and on worse nights. *(He resets the halberd.)* It tells me what you're *willing* to do to get past me. It tells me nothing about *why.* *(He waits.)* So drop the technique. Try me again — but try me with something *true* this time.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He goes very still — caught, and oddly relieved to be.)* ...You see clearly. *(He drops the pretense, and the fear underneath it is bare and human.)* Yes. I'm not keeping you from the Wall. I've been keeping fools *off* it. Three nights now it's been... *waking.* The kept souls press against the stone like the cold presses a window. Names I shouldn't be able to hear, I hear. *(He swallows.)* I've turned back twelve pilgrims to spare them it. *(He looks at you, really looks.)* But you saw through me in one breath, which means you might be the thirteenth I shouldn't turn back. Tell me who you are to the Wall — and I'll decide if you're who it's waking *for.*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He is not a coward — but he is one man, alone, on a causeway, against something in your voice he believes. He steps aside, and hates that he does.)* ...Pass, then. *(stiffly, jaw clenched)* I'll not die at a gate over a dusk-order, and I'll not pretend you didn't make me feel the edge of it. *(as you pass)* But mark me, traveler — you came to a place of the dead and led with a threat. The Wall *remembers* what walks up to it. I hope, for your sake, it remembers you kinder than you've earned in the last minute.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(The threat lands wrong — strikes the bedrock of stubbornness under the fear instead of the fear itself. His knuckles go white and his voice goes quiet, which is worse.)* No. *(He plants himself.)* I have buried men who led with that voice, and I have never once regretted being the gate they didn't get through. *(He raises the halberd to guard.)* You'll turn around now, of your own legs, or you'll learn why a Justiciar of the Doomguide stands a death-watch *alone* and unafraid. *(a beat)* Choose the legs. I'm asking you to choose the legs.

choices.text: [PERSUASION] *(step back; open your hands)* "...That was beneath me, and beneath the night. Forgive it. Let me start again, honestly."
*(Hold your ground. Reach for your weapon.)* "You're alone, Justiciar. That was never a threat. It was a forecast."
*(Turn, and walk back down the causeway.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(A man raised on procedure recognizes another, and some of the wariness becomes something almost like collegiality.)* ...By the book. *(He nods, approving despite himself.)* Few think to ask for the form. They beg, or they bluster. *(He straightens into something more formal.)* Very well: a pilgrim's petition at a closed threshold, properly made, *obliges* the keeper to hear the cause before refusing it. So. State your cause, pilgrim, in proper form — and I am bound, by the same order you're invoking, to weigh it honestly.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He hears the tiredness, and it reaches him more than any argument has.)* No riddle. *(gentler)* I'm not setting you one. *(He sighs.)* But 'please' isn't a cause, and a cause is the one thing the order requires of me before I can let a soul past in the dark. *(He waits, not unkind.)* So give me one. Anything true. Why are *you*, specifically, walking toward a Wall that everyone with sense walks away from?

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(The silence, and the cold rolling off you, is answer enough — and the wrong one. His halberd comes up and his voice goes to the flat, terrible calm of a man who has already decided.)* So that's how it is. The Bone Lord sends his own to the door of the god who *broke* him, on the one night the Wall is stirring, and expects to walk in on the strength of a *chill.* *(He sets his feet.)* No. Not past me. Not tonight. Turn, dead-thing, or be made one properly — I serve the Judge, and the Judge has a *verdict* for what you carry.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He stands ready to hear it — the cause, the true one, the thing that will tip the order one way or the other.)* So. Why have you come to the Wall tonight, of all nights? *(quietly)* And mind — I've heard every lie a frightened soul can tell at a gate. Tell me the thing under the lie instead.

choices.text: [RELIGION] "Because the canon you serve is *wrong* about the Wall, Justiciar — Kelemvor reformed it once, and it can be reformed again. I've come to read whether the hour is now. You, of all servants, should want me to."
[GOOD] "Because someone has to care what happens to the souls no one is allowed to care about. I drew the short straw of being someone. So here I am."
"Because I was told, by someone I trust, that the Wall is changing — and that if it changes unwatched, everyone loses. I came to be the one who watched."
[INTIMIDATION] "My reasons are my own, and your dusk-order doesn't outrank them. Weigh *that* honestly."

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He frowns — not offended, but unconvinced, a careful man finding the seam in an argument.)* Reformed once, reformed again. *(He shakes his head slowly.)* That's a heresy I've heard from gentler mouths than yours, and wanted to believe from all of them. But wanting isn't reading, and you reached past your grasp just now. *(He doesn't raise the halberd — but he doesn't lower it either.)* Give me the *true* reason, not the doctrine you think will move me. I've a better ear for the first than the second.

variants.text: *(Whatever wall was in him comes down all at once, and he speaks to you as he hasn't let himself speak to anyone in six years on this post.)* Then I'll tell you what I've told no one. The Wall is *waking.* Three nights now — the kept souls pressing the stone, names bleeding through where no name should pass. I've turned back a dozen pilgrims to spare them it. *(He stands fully aside, and the relief in it is enormous.)* But you — you I think it's waking *for.* Go up. Read it. And whatever you find that I couldn't — *(his voice cracks, just slightly)* — come back down and tell me there's something to be done. I have been alone with this for three nights and I am so very tired of being the only one who's seen it.

*(He weighs you a final time, and the balance — narrowly — tips.)* ...All right. *(He stands aside, though not without cost.)* The Wall is stirring. I'll say no more than that, because I don't understand more than that, and a Justiciar who guesses out loud is worse than useless. *(He fixes you with a look.)* Go up. Walk careful. And if it *is* changing — be the kind of soul it changes *toward*, not the kind it changes *against.* That much, even the godless can choose.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He has given you a second chance, and the night is too thin for a third.)* Last time, then. *(plainly)* No tags, no doctrine, no leverage. Just the one true sentence under all of it — and I'll know it when I hear it, because true sentences sound different out here. Why are you really walking toward the Wall?

choices.text: [INSIGHT] *(read what he actually needs to hear, and give him that)* "Because you shouldn't have to be the only one who's seen it. Let me up, and you won't be."
"Because I'm grieving, and the thing I'm grieving is up there, and there is nowhere else left for me to go. That's the whole of it."
"...You're right. I don't have a true one I'm willing to give a stranger. I'll go."

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He stands aside fully, and as you pass he says it low, almost to himself — a blessing from a man not sure his god is listening tonight.)* Walk gentle on the causeway. And come back down. *(OUTCOME — He let you through, and he believed in you doing it. The Threshold is open, and you do not climb toward the Wall alone in his thoughts.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He lets you by, but his eyes follow you up the causeway, weighing, unconvinced.)* Go on, then. *(OUTCOME — He opened the gate, but you bought the passage with fear or a lie, not with trust. The Wall, he warned you, remembers how a soul walks up to it — and you climb toward it carrying that.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(You take the gate, and he does not die for it — but something on the causeway *shifts*, a pressure rolling down off the Wall like a held breath let go.)* ...Gods. *(he whispers, staring past you)* You felt that. Tell me you felt that. *(OUTCOME — You forced the Threshold, and fed the waking Wall the first violence of the night. You are through — but you climb toward something now leaning, hungrily, toward you. The flag the Wall keeps on you is not a kind one.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(He holds the line, and the line holds.)* Down the causeway. *(not cruelly, but without yielding)* The Wall will still be here when you've a truer cause, or a better hour, or a god — whichever finds you first. *(OUTCOME — The Threshold stays shut to you tonight. Some gates open only for who you truly are, and tonight, you weren't yet that. Choose a different self in the panel and try the causeway again — that is the whole point of this.)*

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Aldous Vane
nodes.text: *(There is no more talking. He sets his feet, lowers the halberd to guard, and the grey-and-bone of Kelemvor's church stops being a costume and becomes a vow with an edge.)* Then the Judge will weigh us both by morning. *(OUTCOME — It comes to blades at the Threshold. In the full game this is where the dialogue hands off to the combat encounter — the two systems you can preview side by side. Here, the road simply ends in steel. Reset and walk it again as someone he'd have let pass.)*
